vulnerability.title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
vulnerability.description: When the so-called 'sticky bit' is set on a directory, only the owner of a given file may remove that file from the directory. Without the sticky bit, any user with write access to a directory may remove any file in the directory. Setting the sticky bit prevents users from removing each other's files. In cases where there is no reason for a directory to be world-writable, a better solution is to remove that permission rather than to set the sticky bit. However, if a directory is used by a particular application, consult that application's documentation instead of blindly changing modes. To set the sticky bit on a world-writable directory DIR, run the following command: $ sudo chmod +t DIR
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: df --local -P | awk '{if (NR!=1) print $6}' \ | xargs -I '$6' find '$6' -xdev -type d \ \( -perm -0002 -a ! -perm -1000 \) 2>/dev/null \ -exec chmod a+t {} +

triage_decision.reason: Setting the sticky bit on world-writable directories is a simple file permission change that does not affect system boot, authentication, or networking. It is a standard STIG remediation and is unlikely to cause lockout or break critical services.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; applications may rely on existing directory permissions; potential for misconfiguration if applied to non‑world‑writable directories

review_feedback: The proposed command contains errors (using find instead of df) and lacks proper handling of mount point names with spaces, so it will not correctly locate and fix world‑writable directories. It also omits error redirection and may unintentionally affect non‑local filesystems.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed command contains errors (using find instead of df) and lacks proper handling of mount point names with spaces, so it will not correctly locate and fix world‑writable directories. It also omits error redirection and may unintentionally affect non‑local filesystems.
concerns: Potential issues with mount points containing spaces
Command may run on non‑local filesystems if df output includes them
No verification step before applying chmod
suggested_improvements: Use null‑terminated output (df -P0) and xargs -0 to handle spaces in mount points
Consider using chmod +t instead of a+t for clarity
Test the find command without -exec first to confirm target directories
